title: Śroṇasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: core material likely 1st millennium BCE oral tradition; redacted within the early Buddhist canon, exact date uncertain
summary: A canonical Buddhist discourse (sūtra) connected with the disciple Śroṇa (Pali Soṇa, often Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa), an important early follower of the Buddha. Such texts typically narrate the conversion, ordination, or instruction of the named disciple and present a point of doctrine or monastic discipline through that episode.
history: Śroṇa Koṭīkarṇa is a well-attested figure in early Buddhist literature (e.g. the Divyāvadāna and the Vinaya traditions), famed for requesting modifications of monastic rules for border regions. The precise sūtra intended by this short title is not firmly identifiable, so its identity is held with reservation.

title: Sukhāvatyamṛtavyūha (Sukhāvatī[amṛta]vyūhasūtra), the Smaller Sukhāvatīvyūha
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 1st-2nd c. CE
summary: The shorter of the two principal Pure Land sūtras describing the buddha-field (Sukhāvatī) of the Buddha Amitābha. The Buddha praises the splendor and bliss of that western paradise and teaches that beings who hear and keep Amitābha's name with single-minded faith will be reborn there. It is one of the foundational scriptures of Pure Land Buddhism.
history: Composed in Sanskrit, probably in northwest India (Gandhāra region), and translated into Chinese repeatedly between c. 240 and 400 CE, most famously by Kumārajīva in 402; one Tibetan translation exists from the 8th-9th c. by Dānaśīla and Yeshé Dé. The Sanskrit text survives alongside Chinese and Tibetan versions.

title: Tathāgatabimbakārāpaṇasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: mid 1st millennium CE (uncertain)
summary: A short Mahayana sūtra on the merit of commissioning and establishing images of the Buddha (tathāgata-bimba). The Buddha enumerates the benefits accruing to a person who sponsors the making of a Buddha image, such as prosperity, beauty, wealth, royal status and favorable rebirths.
history: Preserved in the Tibetan Kangyur (sūtra section) under titles meaning 'The Benefits of Establishing Images of the Noble Tathāgata.' It belongs to a genre of merit-extolling sūtras that supported the cult of image-making and donation in Buddhist communities.

title: Vṛkṣasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
tradition: uncertain; possibly Buddhist or secular/scientific (Vṛkṣāyurveda tradition)
date_estimate: uncertain
summary: A short text whose title means 'aphorisms on trees.' If it belongs to the Indian science of plant life (vṛkṣāyurveda), it would treat tree anatomy, planting, soil, watering, propagation and the treatment of plant diseases. The exact work intended here cannot be confidently determined.
history: A 'Vṛkṣasūtra' / Vṛkṣāyurveda tradition is associated with figures such as Sūrapāla (c. 10th-11th c.) and is excerpted in works like the Bṛhatsaṃhitā of Varāhamihira and the Agni Purāṇa. Because this entry is catalogued among Buddhist (K10) sūtras, its identification is ambiguous and held with low confidence.

title: Adhyardhaśatikā Prajñāpāramitā (Adhyardhaśatikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra)
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 7th-8th c. CE (tantric Prajñāpāramitā layer)
summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 150 Lines,' a short esoteric Prajñāpāramitā scripture. It presents the perfection of wisdom through a tantric framework, with chapters spoken by various buddhas, each expounding a method of prajñāpāramitā summarized by a mystic syllable. It is a key text of esoteric Buddhism and remains widely recited in Japanese Shingon.
history: Belonging to the late, tantric phase of Prajñāpāramitā literature, it bridges sūtra and tantra. It was transmitted to East Asia and became central to Shingon practice; Tibetan and Chinese versions also exist.

title: Sitātapatrā
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: second half of 1st millennium CE
summary: The Sitātapatrā ('White Parasol') Uṣṇīṣa dhāraṇī, a protective scripture centered on the goddess Sitātapatrā, said to have emerged from the uṣṇīṣa (crown protuberance) of the Buddha while in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three. The dhāraṇī is apotropaic, guarding reciters against supernatural dangers, evil spirits and harm.
history: Sitātapatrā became a fully developed protective deity venerated in both Mahayana and Vajrayana traditions, with multi-armed iconographic forms. Related dhāraṇī material is embedded in the Śūraṅgama Sūtra; the text is preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan and Chinese.

title: Bhaiṣajyaguruvaidūryaprabharājasūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 2nd-7th c. CE; Chinese translation by 317 CE
summary: The sūtra of Bhaiṣajyaguru, the 'Medicine Master and Lapis Lazuli Light King' (the Medicine Buddha). It recounts his twelve great vows made as a bodhisattva to heal illness, relieve suffering and guide beings to enlightenment, and prescribes devotional and ritual practices for healing and protection.
history: Sanskrit manuscripts survive from Gilgit and Bāmiyān (no later than the 7th c.); the earliest Chinese translation was by Śrīmitra in 317 CE, and the cult spread along the Silk Road into China, Tibet and East Asia. It is a foundational text for the widespread Medicine Buddha devotion.

title: Suvarṇaprabhāsasūtra (Suvarṇaprabhāsottamasūtrendrarāja)
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: reached current form by c. 4th-5th c. CE; oldest strata 1st-2nd c. CE
summary: The 'Golden Light Sūtra,' a highly influential Mahayana scripture. It combines confession and purification practices, teachings on emptiness and the Buddha's lifespan, and chapters in which protective deities (e.g. Sarasvatī, Śrī, the Four Great Kings) vow to guard kings and realms who honor the sūtra, making it a key text of Buddhist statecraft and protection.
history: Originally Sanskrit, translated into Chinese by Dharmakṣema (early 5th c.) and most completely by Yijing in 703 CE, and later into Tibetan and other languages. It became one of the most popular and politically important Mahayana sūtras across Central and East Asia.

title: Mañjuśrīmūlakalpa (Āryamañjuśrīmūlakalpa)
author: anonymous/traditional
genre: tantra / ritual manual (kalpa) with sutra framing
date_estimate: core c. 6th c. CE; compiled and expanded through the 8th c. CE and after
summary: The 'Root Manual of the Rites of Mañjuśrī,' a large ritual compendium affiliated with the bodhisattva Mañjuśrī. Across fifty-five chapters the Buddha sets out mantras, maṇḍalas, consecrations (including vase consecration), iconography, astrology and rites for worldly and soteriological ends. It also famously contains a verse 'prophecy' section used as a quasi-historical source for early medieval Indian dynasties.
history: Often cited as the earliest extant Indian Buddhist tantra. Scholars date a core verse text to around the 6th c. with the bulk compiled through the 8th c. (Matsunaga, Sanderson) and accretions thereafter; in Tibetan classification it is a Kriyā-tantra.

title: Svalpākṣarā Prajñāpāramitā
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: later 1st millennium CE (tantric Prajñāpāramitā phase)
summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in a Few Syllables/Words,' a short esoteric Prajñāpāramitā scripture in which Avalokiteśvara is a chief speaker. It condenses the perfection of wisdom into a brief form combined with mantra/dhāraṇī, intended for recitation by those unable to study the larger Prajñāpāramitā corpus.
history: One of about forty Prajñāpāramitā texts produced in India, belonging to the later, condensed and tantric-flavored group of such works. It is preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan and Chinese.

title: Kauśikaprajñāpāramitāsūtra
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: extant Sanskrit/Chinese recension late 10th-early 11th c. CE; earlier Indian core
summary: A condensed Prajñāpāramitā sūtra named for Kauśika (an epithet of Indra), to whom the Buddha addresses it. The Buddha equates the characteristics of the perfection of wisdom with those of all phenomena, the five aggregates and elements, stressing the nonduality of conventional phenomena and emptiness; it incorporates mantra and is classed among esoteric Prajñāpāramitā texts.
history: Brief (about four Tibetan folios), it blends sūtra and tantra and is variously placed in both the Prajñāpāramitā and Tantra sections of the Tibetan Kangyur. A longer Central Asian version with additional mantras also exists; the extant Sanskrit largely matches the late Chinese translation.

title: Adhyardhaśatikā Prajñāpāramitā (Adhyardhaśatikāprajñāpāramitāsūtra)
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: c. 7th-8th c. CE (tantric Prajñāpāramitā layer)
summary: The 'Perfection of Wisdom in 150 Lines,' a short esoteric Prajñāpāramitā scripture presenting the perfection of wisdom through a tantric framework, with chapters spoken by various buddhas and summarized by mystic syllables. (This entry duplicates the same work as K12adhsatpu in this batch.)
history: A late, tantric Prajñāpāramitā text bridging sūtra and tantra; it became central to Japanese Shingon practice and is preserved in Sanskrit, Tibetan and Chinese.

title: Dhvajāgrakeyūrā-dhāraṇī
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: second half of 1st millennium CE
summary: A protective (apotropaic) dhāraṇī set in the Heaven of the Thirty-Three, where Śakra, defeated by the asuras, asks the Buddha for help and is given this spell. Reciters become victorious in conflict, and those who fix the formula atop banners or tie it on the body are guarded by the goddess Dhvajāgrakeyūrā, who removes fear and grants protection and prosperity.
history: A dhāraṇī of the state-protection genre whose formula was personified as a goddess; the text instructs followers to inscribe the long dhāraṇī on banner-flags. (This is one of two identical entries in this batch.)

title: Dhvajāgrakeyūrā-dhāraṇī
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: second half of 1st millennium CE
summary: A protective dhāraṇī in which Śakra, lord of the gods, defeated by the asuras, receives this spell from the Buddha. The goddess Dhvajāgrakeyūrā grants victory in conflict, removes fear and bestows protection and abundance on those who recite the formula or fasten it to banners or the body.
history: Belongs to the Buddhist apotropaic/state-protection dhāraṇī tradition, with the protective formula personified as a goddess inscribed on banner-flags. (This entry is a duplicate of K12dhvajkdu in this batch.)

title: Ekādaśamukhahṛdaya
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: by c. 6th c. CE (Chinese translation c. 570 CE)
summary: The 'Heart-dhāraṇī of the Eleven-Faced Avalokiteśvara' (Avalokiteśvara-ekādaśamukha-hṛdaya-dhāraṇī-sūtra), centered on a potent dhāraṇī of the eleven-faced form of the compassionate bodhisattva Avalokiteśvara. It promises protection, healing and spiritual advancement; the eleven faces symbolize the bodhisattva's simultaneous observation of the ten directions and beyond.
history: An early esoteric scripture: a Chinese translation by Yaśogupta appeared around 570 CE and another by Xuanzang in 656 CE. The chant remains popular throughout East Asia and is preserved in Sanskrit, Chinese and Tibetan.

title: Gaṇapatihṛdaya-Dhāraṇī
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: 1st millennium CE (uncertain); transmitted widely by the medieval period
summary: The 'Exalted Heart-dhāraṇī of Gaṇapati,' spoken by the Buddha on Vulture Peak to Ānanda. It teaches a heart-mantra of Gaṇapati (the Buddhist form of Gaṇeśa, lord of the gaṇas): whoever upholds and recites it daily will succeed in all endeavors, gain wealth and prosperity, and avoid poverty and suffering.
history: Preserved in both the Tantra Collection and the Compendium of Dhāraṇīs of the Tibetan Kangyur. In Nepalese practice it is one of the saptavāra ('seven days') dhāraṇīs, associated with a particular weekday; in Buddhist tradition Gaṇapati is treated as a worldly protector or, in some Mahayana accounts, an emanation of Avalokiteśvara.

title: Grahamātṛkānāmadhāraṇī
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: materialized in current form in the second half of the 1st millennium CE
summary: The 'Dhāraṇī of the Mother of Planets,' a short ritual text for protection against malign planetary (graha) influences. It sets out a ritual framework with visualizations and offerings to the nine planets in anthropomorphic form, each with its own mantras, creating a protective astral schema for the practitioner.
history: Very popular across North India, Central Asia and Tibet in the later first millennium, it reflects the assimilation of Brahmanical planetary worship into Buddhist ritual. Surviving manuscripts are late (no earlier than 1492 CE), but the text itself is considerably older; the Tibetan translation belongs to the early medieval period.

title: Mahādaṇḍadhāraṇīśītavatī
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: Tibetan translation c. 800 CE; Indian composition earlier in the 1st millennium CE
summary: An apotropaic Buddhist scripture, the 'Great Staff Dhāraṇī, the Cool One' (Mahā-Daṇḍadhāraṇī-Śītavatī). It provides protective dhāraṇīs against dangers such as snakebite, illness and malign forces, with recitation practices including the use of knotted protective threads.
history: Its title evolved in transmission from Mahā-Daṇḍadhāraṇī toward Mahāśītavatī, the name under which it was incorporated as one member of the Pañcarakṣā ('Five Protections') collection. Over 300 Pañcarakṣā manuscripts survive, chiefly from Nepal and eastern India; the Tibetan translation dates to around 800 CE.

title: Mahāpratisarāmahāvidyārājñī
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: first half of the 7th c. CE
summary: The 'Great Amulet, Great Queen of Spells,' one of the most popular Buddhist protective dhāraṇī texts. It contains two dhāraṇīs and several mantras with detailed instructions for their recitation and for making amulets, promising protection from dangers, disease and misfortune to those who wear or recite them.
history: Datable to the first half of the 7th c., with five fragmentary Gilgit birch-bark manuscripts as its earliest witnesses. It is the first of the five goddesses of the Pañcarakṣā collection and was enormously widespread, illustrating the evolution of dhāraṇīs in tantric Buddhism from the 6th-8th centuries; it was used as a worn talisman across Asia.

title: Mahāmaṇivipulavimānaviśvasupratiṣṭhitaguhyaparamarahasyakalparājadhāraṇī
author: anonymous/traditional
date_estimate: mid-to-later 1st millennium CE
summary: A tantric dhāraṇī-scripture (kalparāja) associated with a great jewel-pavilion (mahāmaṇivipulavimāna), presenting a secret, supreme dhāraṇī together with rites and benefits. Such 'secret essence king of manuals' texts give the dhāraṇī plus instructions for its ritual use, including consecration, stūpa deposit and protection.
history: Known in Chinese as the 大寶廣博樓閣善住祕密陀羅尼經 (the 'Secret Dhāraṇī of the Well-Established Great-Jewel Vast Pavilion'). It circulated in Sanskrit, Chinese and Tibetan and belongs to the esoteric dhāraṇī/kalpa literature used in consecration and stūpa rites.
